The way that we really think about ourselves is that we are an AI training platform.
The primary feature that we bring to bear to that today is our AI role plays tool.
AI role plays tool is, and Nick would give a much better pitch here, but, or me on a healthy day.
The way that we sort of generally frame this up is we say something like, you know, we've been doing learning and development as practitioners for over 10 years.
And when we talk to leaders and executives, we ask, you know, do you wish your team was better trained?

Like, if you could train your team, could wave a magic wand and change their behaviors in particular, or inculcate a new skill, or have them become more knowledgeable about some topic, would you do that?
And whether we're talking to HR leaders, or sales leaders, or customer success or customer support leaders, all of them are like, oh, yeah, like, my team has gaps all over the place.
So we say, okay, cool, like, and, and how much are you investing in, in solving those problems?
And the answer is always like, well, you I'd like to invest, but I can't, because I just don't really believe in the modalities of training.
You know, I've done courses, I've, you know, we do these things, we do a little bit, but it just doesn't really work.
And fundamentally, we hear this over and over, which is that training is really hard to make stick.
It's really hard to actually change behavior.
And what we've learned as practitioners is that there's really two reasons for that.
And the first one is just around modalities.
So there's modalities that work really well.
Think human, one-on-one, really high-intensive coaching, like your manager watching you and coaching you and giving you feedback.
That stuff works really well, but it's very hard to scale and it's very expensive.
On the other side, there's very scalable things like courses or videos or read this article or watch this.
That stuff scales really well, but it doesn't really change behavior.
And there is this thing in the field that's called the learning-doing gap, which is, sure, you learn the material by watching it or reading the article and you're like, yeah, I got it.
You did the quiz to prove that you have 10-second knowledge retention.
But when you go to do it tomorrow or the next week or, God forbid, next month, that's totally gone from your brain.
And it's all very grounded in human psychology.
Is your brain just forgets stuff that didn't have an emotional impact on you.
And so for a really long time, there just wasn't a solution for this.

Last year in 2024, it became possible to use technology to solve this problem where you could get both the effectiveness of that really intensive human, that human coaching, but the scalability of content.
And that's what role plays does.
It's what we bring to bear.
So we bring very realistic characters in realistic scenarios that are all very customized for your business.
And we put your people in there to do the practice scenarios.
And most importantly, the doing of the scenario is valuable because it triggers a stress response in the brain.
And that stress response is very important for causing your synapses to reform and cause actual real learning to happen.
That's how behavior actually changes.
So you close the learning-doing gap.
But more important than that, we also have a feedback loop for

So as an organization, there's standards that you're trying to set, or there's learning goals that you're trying to hit, and we encode those in the platform as rubric items that the scenario then gets assessed against.

So immediately after doing the session, the person who did the session gets really detailed feedback explaining what they did well, what they should have done, or what they didn't do so well, what they could have done instead to do better.
And of course, all of that then becomes bubbled up into analytics for the management staff, so that they can see how is my team doing, where are the gaps, where do we need to invest more.
And this is super helpful in a couple of different places in an organization.
So companies use it primarily for onboarding, so new hire, they need to get them up to speed with the current organizational standards.
Two is new product releases.
So you're launching a new product, you want your sales team to be conversation ready on the day of the product launch, not sick.
A month after six months later after flubbing it a bunch of times.
And then the other ones are around organizational transformation.
So for example, you might as an organization say, hey, we're changing the way that we do feedback.
OK, cool.
So we're going to retrain all the managers in the new feedback model.
And they can actually do it as opposed to just sit through another hour long lecture while they read their email.
So this is the three ones.
It's really onboarding, new releases, transformation projects.
Those are sort of the places that we play the best.
